title: Front Porch Time: An American Tradition We Need to Revive
gist: John A. Cuddeback argues that the near-disappearance of the front porch from American life symbolizes a broader loss of 'the ordinary' — daily unstructured togetherness, storytelling, and household work — and, drawing on his new book The Intentional Household, urges families to deliberately rebuild 'porch-time' and 'story-time' as antidotes to social isolation.

themes.theme: Porch-time as the ordinary that sustains family life

insights.claim: Cuddeback coins the term 'porch-time' to describe a category of everyday household activities — being together, talking, working with one's hands, sharing meals — that constitute ordinary human flourishing and were historically centered on the front porch.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete, nameable concept (porch-time) that family-formation and community organizations could use as a program or messaging framework to rebuild social capital.
insights.quote: Porch-time stands for the ‘ordinary’ in human life in the best sense of the term

insights.claim: Journalist Michael Dolan, researching his book The American Porch, found that front-porch culture — which many assume is uniquely American — actually recurs across many historical epochs and cultures.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Complicates the 'porches are quintessentially American' framing often used in nostalgia-driven home-design and lifestyle marketing.
insights.quote: what he thought was as American as “a greenback dollar” turned out to be instantiated across epochs and cultures

insights.claim: UNC Chapel Hill professor Harry Watson recalls that while working as a door-to-door salesman in Alabama, he observed people sitting on their front porches shelling beans for dinner around 4 p.m. every afternoon — an example of porches structuring daily communal routine.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Illustrates concretely how porch-centered routines once organized daily life, a benchmark for what a 'together time' revival could look like today.
insights.quote: I went around town, and every afternoon by about 4 o’clock, people would be on the front porch shelling beans for dinner.

insights.claim: An 1852 architecture manual by Lewis Allen described southern verandas as central to daily comfort — used for exercise in wet weather, recreation during summer heat, taking meals, and receiving visitors — with some households living almost entirely under their shade.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Provides historical grounding for how central porch space once was to household life, useful context for the current 'reviving front porches' design and lifestyle trend.
insights.quote: no feature of the house in a southern climate can be more expressive of easy, comfortable enjoyment than a spacious veranda

insights.claim: Cuddeback argues American society has largely abandoned or lost 'the ordinary' — routine, unstructured togetherness — by gravitating toward 'the new, the glitzy, the progressive,' and that this drift now requires active defense rather than being taken for granted.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Frames declining everyday communal practice as a driver of social fragmentation — relevant to any thesis linking eroded informal socializing ('third places') to family and community breakdown.
insights.quote: as a society we have largely abandoned or lost the ordinary as we gravitate to the new, the glitzy, the progressive

insights.claim: Southern Agrarian writer Andrew Lyttle warned nearly a century ago, in the essay collection I'll Take My Stand, that it had already become necessary to defend 'the ordinary' before it vanished entirely — suggesting this erosion is a recurring, long-running cultural pattern rather than a new phenomenon.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Contextualizes the current 'revive community' moment as part of a century-long recurring cycle, tempering expectations that any single intervention will be a permanent fix.
insights.locator: Andrew Lyttle warned almost a century ago

insights.claim: Cuddeback frames leisure and work as mutually reinforcing rather than opposed, noting that historically porch-time often combined the two, such as doing handwork like shelling beans while socializing.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Challenges a leisure-vs-productivity binary underlying much modern time-use and wellness discourse — relevant to framing 'quality time' interventions as compatible with chores rather than requiring separate blocked-off time.
insights.quote: true leisure and good work are connected at their roots, each arising from and leading back to the other

insights.claim: In his new book The Intentional Household: Living as if People Matter, Cuddeback prescribes concrete household action steps, starting with deliberately 'making together time' and 'making story time' as the foundation for renewing family and home life.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: A packaged, actionable framework (a book plus a podcast, 'The Intentional Household') that family-focused media or programs could adopt or cite as a practical intervention.

insights.claim: Cuddeback argues storytelling — family history, religious scripture, literature — is central to leisure because it lets people situate themselves within a larger narrative; in Jewish and Christian tradition, he notes, Scripture functions as 'the narrative that is the context of all narratives.'
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: Links religious and cultural narrative transmission directly to family cohesion, relevant to research on the connection between religiosity and family stability.
insights.quote: the Scriptures provide a narrative that is the context of all narratives

insights.claim: Cuddeback cites country singer Tracy Lawrence's lyric about wishing the world still had 'a front porch like we did back then' as evidence that the front-porch ideal still carries broad emotional resonance and nostalgia even among people unfamiliar with the lived tradition.
insights.speaker: John A. Cuddeback
insights.so_what: A soft indicator of consumer and cultural appetite for porch-centric home design and lifestyle content, already present in mainstream country music.
insights.quote: If the world had a front porch like we did back then . . .
